text.title: ALBANIA SEGRETA - Tatuatori in bunker antiatomici e spie comuniste
text.u: [Giuseppe] Do we dare?
[music]
This may be the most secret Bunker in all of Albania.
Hey, you motherf***er!
It's going to be a long day...
I asked if he could give me a tattoo,
and now he's picking one.
My name is Giuseppe and I have a mission:
to travel the world,
to meet the most extraordinary people of the planet
and to ask them a simple question:
"What does happiness mean to you?"
Welcome to Progetto Happiness.
[music]
Incredible!
I'm inside the biggest, securest bunker in the world.
I'm 300 feet below Tirana, Albania's capital.
Albania holds a saddening record:
it has more bunkers than any other country.
In the 70s, the paranoia of Albania's communist former dictator Enver Hoxha
resulted in the nationwide construction of more than 170,000 bunkers.
Hoxha was one of the worst despots of the East.
For 40 years he led the country with an iron fist,
isolated Albania from the rest of the world,
making it into a kind of North Korea.
He enacted extremist communist policies,
doing away with private property, freedom of speech and religion,
condemning the Albanians to live inside an open-air prison
without any window to the outside world.
From the post-war period to the 1990s,
Albanians lived in terror of the Sigurimi, a frightening secret police,
and bunkers were the result of Hoxha's manic obsession
with an unlikely enemy attack.
After the fall of the communist regime in the '90s,
many bunkers were destroyed to forget that sad moment.
But others remained to commemorate
the resilience of the Albanian people.
We'll go around all of Albania to find the most iconic bunkers,
meet someone who experienced firsthand
the dictatorship of the communist regime,
and understand what it means today to be happy in Albania.
It's my first time in Albania. I've never been.
I was surprised to find out *only* 3 million people live here.
A fairly small number,
but Albanian pride is immeasurable.
They're very proud of their nation, their ethnicity...
And they don't call themselves "Albanians",
but rather "Shqiptarë",
which means "Children of the Eagles".
and in turn, Albania is the "Nest of the Eagles".
This is also apparent in the national flag:
a red background and a two-headed eagle.
Very proud.
We see it everywhere, in all houses
there's always the flag waving.
[music]
A bunker!
Usually bunkers are found in the countryside.
Do we dare?
There it is.
This definitely is someone's land. It's been cultivated.
Usually bunkers this big are only found on private property.
Public ones have been dismantled since the regime's fall
to recover the iron and sell it.
And inside such big bunkers there's a ton of iron.
But this one's still standing.
Let's go look.
Many Albanians hate this symbol of the regime,
while others have left them standing as a momento,
as a reminder to those who did not live through it
that there was a very dark period of Albanian history.
Interesting...
Now everything is destroyed, abandoned.
Nothing but a fig plant.
The dome-shaped structure...
to protect against "potential" enemy attacks
which never actually occurred.
Many of my Albanian friends told me
that nowadays the bunkers are used,
or were used, once the regime fell,
as public toilets, if you get my point,
or for a roll in the hay.
A safe, protected place to bring girls...
And make love.
Holy cow!
These are much bigger than what we just saw.
It's like a post-apocalyptic scene.
As if we were the last to survive on Earth...
and these bunkers saved our lives.
But no, these were bunkers they believed necessary
in case of a potential attack, probably from the Adriatic Sea.
They were strategically positioned, in front of the sea
to look out for the arrival of possible enemy ships...
But they never arrived.
[music]
Albania is so beautiful!
During the communist era, Enver Hoxha declared Albania
the first atheist state in the world.
Indeed, the only religion allowed was "Albanianism",
the cult of the fatherland.
Secret police closed down all places of worship
and began to persecute believers of every religion.
But one policeman, whose very job
was to spy on others, did something extraordinary.
We are going to meet him.
As we prepared for the war, we were ready to defend ourselves
from the imperialists, the Americans,
the Russians, Germans, British... everyone.
And all our preparation was in how to intercept signals
to understand what was happening,
whether enemies were attacking us or not.
And coincedentally, in those years,
I was listening to a transmission from Monte Carlo in Albanian.
It caught my attention.
It was on shortwave signals and talked about someone named Jesus.
And they said he was a savior,
that by believing in him, your life will be saved,
and he will give you a new life, a life of plenty.
After several messages, I decided to listen to it again,
in the next transmission and going forward.
The problem is that it was very dangerous for me
listening to it regularly while working in the military.
I couldn't listen because it was illegal,
and I would have wound up either losing my job,
being persecuted, or put in prison.
In the time of communism, we were taught about only one person.
We only knew the dictator Enver Hoxha.
He was a God to us, the only one who could save us.
We have been raised from childhood to praise him,
saying "Glory to Hoxha", all of that kind of thing.
This made me very curious about the word of Jesus.
Learning of another hero whose name I'd never heard,
who we didn't grow up with and couldn't even imagine.
We might have heard the name "God", but not "Jesus".
These short messages on the radio were very clear,
very simple and understandable for us.
And that got me started on listening every day,
writing, and learning to find out who this savior is.
Not that he would have saved me then,
but I wanted to compare him to the one we lived for, Jesus.
God says: "I will open the eyes of the blind."
I understood what real life was, thank God.
Maybe my friends were uncomfortable,
some close friends have renounced me,
and they asked me what I was doing, but I was very happy.
My happiness is different.
What we need to understand is that I am no longer the old Berti.
I've changed, my life has changed, now it makes sense.
and my happiness is that I no longer have love of this world,
but I have the love of Christ, and I'm glad that not just me,
but many others have heard the message,
and I continue to serve them.
Because we know how lost we were.
But now we are happy, we will be together with Christ,
with Jesus today, and for eternity.
Half a century of ideological madness which denied the rights of God
and systematically denying human rights
did not manage to eliminate the hope of the Albanians,
who, as in the case of Berti, clung to their faith.
This may be the most secret Bunker in all of Albania.
It was reserved for the high ranks of the Albanian army.
Nobody knew it existed apart from the Army.
When the communist regime fell, it was discovered by chance:
a lady bought this land, built a hotel on it
and one day she found a secret tunnel
leading to the other side of the hill.
[music]
There are bats here. See?
- No. - Look...
-I can't see. -Ooh!
Here it is, here it is...
[bats squeaking]
It touched me...
[laughs]
-Did you see it? -Yes!
It's a huge bunker.
Hundreds of soldiers could barricade here,
and leaders who would command from here.
These rooms were used both as bedrooms and offices.
Where they would send communications to the troops.
The doors were also made of reinforced concrete
to not allow radiation to enter in case of nuclear attack.
In this way, whoever was hiding here
would have survived even an atomic bomb.
The owner was very kind.
She doesn't let anyone in this place.
And she just showed me the tunnel
that connected the district headquarters to the bunker.
From here the soldiers could hide in case of attack.
And now it's a hotel, and she is the owner.
She was very kind.
What incredible luck.
Small interruption from Future Giuseppe.
Right now I am in Skopje, Macedonia.
I'm here to tell you an amazing story:
the city of the Romani people.
Where the mayor, newspapers, and even the television is all Roma.
But in September I'll tell you everything.
I come from the future to tell you a secret,
one of my travel secrets.
I drink when I'm thirsty.
I know, amazing.
But you must ask yourself:
*what* do I drink?
I drink AirUp.
This water bottle is brilliant.
Thanks to these pods with different natural flavors,
while I drink, I breathe through my nose
and the aroma of the pod travels from my throat to my nose,
making me feel like I'm drinking water flavored with wild berries,
when really it's just plain water.
This way I have more desire to drink during the day,
especially now that it's summer,
I avoid sugary drinks and, above all,
I don't waste plastic bottles during my travels.
Now that I've revealed one of my most amazing secrets,
I'll leave a link below to try AirUp.
Now back to Albania for an interest factoid for us Italians.
One of the most curious consequences
of the communist era and its insane censorship
is that in Albania 70% of people speak Italian.
I know it sounds absurd, but until 1991,
year of the fall of the regime,
the only way for them to have news from the world
was to turn their antennas towards our country,
to tune in secretly to Italian radio and TV.
We were their only window to the world.
That's what I was told by a construction worker
who I met by chance, and bought me a coffee.
Italy. "Do you speak Italian too?"
Even the cats speak Italian here!
Good?
It's not Lavazza...
How did you feel when you watched
the world outside Albania through Italian television?
Secretly, we watch Rai 1 and Rai 2...
Secretly.
Because the Sigurimi…
-Yes, the secret police... -Secret police.
They roamed all over the city, in all neighborhoods...
And in all the alleys to see if our antennas were facing Italy.
My God!
It hurts me, the memory...
Their faces, always like this.
It was a nightmare!
- A nightmare? - Yes!
I said: "But why was all this necessary?"
The whole world lives free.
Back then, no.
"Don't look over there, don't watch TV,"
"Do not go..." All closed off.
The regime did not allow us to read anything
apart from the history the Russians wrote.
We didn't like it, we wanted the West.
And who better than Italy?
Here, in front of us.
So... Fashion, literature,
the poetry, the songs...
Culture...
Italy has a lot to give to humanity.
Hearing Beznic speak about my home country
with so much admiration affects me deeply,
because I've never seen Italy with those eyes.
I probably always have taken it for granted.
And before saying goodbye I have to thank him
for reminding me of the luck I have
in being able to call it "home".
We are about to get to the most amazing bunker of all
it is absurd what they managed to create
inside a fallout bunker!
It's a tattoo studio!
In there a person, a character,
who's now a local legend,
created a studio in which he tattoos people,
his name is Keq Mark, and it's a name that tells a story
because Keq means "Bad".
He has a somewhat murky past,
because he spent 9 years in jail in New York
and then he was deported here to Albania.
Oh, we're here!
Okay, so: from now on
really anything can happen.
He's very...
histrionic, I've already been told.
It could be good or bad...
Just think that he decides whether to tattoo his clients or not,
and if he doesn't like you you can go back home...
And he also decides what to tattoo.
So...
Fingers crossed and let's go get Keq.
Nice to meet you, Giuseppe.
They call me Peter, but my nickname is Keq.
With square haircut and a cold and motionless gaze,
Keq looks like a Soviet villain from an American movie.
But really it only takes a few seconds to reveal his welcoming nature.
He wants to show me his famous bunker right away,
furnished with his art: apocalyptic,
provocative and somehow spiritual.
[music]
Would you prefer me to call you Keq or Peter?
Keq.
Because Keq is something bad.
How did you manage to escape and go to the United States?
I went to Yugoslavia,
I applied for a visa with the American Embassy,
and I went to America.
And what did you do in America?
How did you live?
I used to work as a pizza maker.
I was paid well, but then…
Yeah, I happened to meet other three guys
who used to stay in Belgrade in Yugoslavia with me
and they had no place to stay so I took them in.
And the landlord told me
that I couldn't bring anyone so I told the guys:
"When you walk down the stairs don't f*****g make noise."
And they listened to me for a day or two
then they forgot and they kicked me out.
I lost my job and everything and I ended up on the street.
And I got locked up for armed robbery.
Tell me the truth, did you like it? Was it exciting?
Yes, it was like an addiction.
Like adrenaline.
You know, I mean...
I was not afraid when they called me to work.
They'd say, "Hey man, we have a job. Come over."
I didn't care if I had to be the lookout, go in, or... whatever.
So many times...
I was seconds away from death.
How did you end up here with this bunker?
When I got married I bought this land.
The bunker happened to be here,
so I decided to use it for something.
I started tattooing as a hobby,
people started liking it and we are close to the main road.
So many people from different nations
stopped by to take pictures.
I got to talking with them,
So I said, "F**k it, I have to do something with that."
So now I'm making a living out of that.
In addition to tattoos, Keq's main passion is guns.
He wants to try one out with me which he assembled himself.
Holy s**t!
Go this way, turn back.
So I have to go a little bit...
Yes, keep going.
He's fucking drunk.
He is my barber!
Hey, you motherf***er!
Johnny!
You guys wanna have a drink here?
Why not.
Come on.
I don't know what's going on.
We are going to have a drink with him,
I think he's already had a lot...
Yes.
He is Keq's friend.
Keq's friend!
It's going to be a long day, I think...
Beer?
Beer.
At 11 in the morning...
Thank you very much.
He's drunk too.
Another one.
I like the Italian language...
Do you speak Italian, a little?
No.
What a sight!
He was very drunk too...
He's in the middle of the street. I'm worried about him.
Albania is the best country in the Balkans.
Why?
We have the best nature, the best lakes,
the best oceans, beaches. You understand?
Keq is taking me to the lookout point
from which Enver Hoxha, back in the days of communism,
admired what he believed to be his empire.
He would stop here to smoke for 10-15 minutes.
And check this view, always.
Imagine what he was thinking. "Everything is mine."
Yes.
They were so poor but ready to die for the country.
Ready to die.
Would you?
Well... probably yes, even now I would die for my country.
When you talk about jail , it seems almost..
not fun, but something that is ok.
Yeah, 'cause you get used to that shit, man.
Yeah.
You get used to it.
You know what the hardest part is...
When are you get arrested, for example?
The hardest part is when you get sentenced
and the judge with the hammer "5 years, 6 years, 10 years..."
Then you have to settle down because you have nothing.
You can't change anything, you gotta f*****g do the time.
Forget about it.
Work out, keep busy, draw, do something.
You understand?
How many jails did you "visit"?
I've probably been to 20 jails.
20 jails?
Yes.
I was in Auburn one time, the oldest prison in America.
I was in maximum security.
And one time I remember I had two days left in my sentence.
And there was this black guy who was in prison for life.
I was listening to my Walkman
and he asks me if he can use it for a couple of hours
I knew he would never give it back to me.
But I felt that if I didn't give it to him,
I would have been a f*****g weakling.
So I gave it to him, but then…
I had a fight with him. Two days left on my sentence.
We fought knife to knife.
How did you get the knife?
You can take a piece of steel, sharpen it...
you make a knife with anything.
Because when your mind works like this,
you can always make something out of that,
you understand?
And you always knew you'd have to defend yourself.
Yes, but...
Even though I was two days away fom finishing my sentence
they guy's got a life sentence,
I won't lend you my f*****g Walkman, I'd rather fight.
I swear to God, I'm not lying.
Keq, can I see your tattoos?
Yes why not?
Do you like the idea?
I have a total of 80... 89/90.
All by yourself?
Yes, with the left hand, with the right.
My son did my back.
- Your son did it? - Yeah.
It says "Apocalypse".
Which is your favorite?
I have some on my legs.
Let me see if I have underwear on, sometimes I don't wear 'em.
This is the devil in a glass. The woman holding the glass.
The only part you didn't do was your back.
That's right, I couldn't do it. You know what I thought?
I swear to God, I won't lie to you.
I had the tattoo gun and I thought of hanging it on the wall...
No...
Stick it to the wall, let the engine run
and take a mirror like this...
But you didn't do it.
No, I didn't do it, because...
That would have been funny, though.
But either way, you know...
This is my property, I can do whatever the f*** I want.
I don't want hi-tech tattoos.
I did these in prison, so I don't care.
I like them the way they are.
They're a little bad, but I got them in prison.
They have a history behind them.
I asked Keq if he could give me a tattoo
I waited for his reply for a few seconds...
I wanted to understand whether he wanted to or not.
Finally he said yes, and now he's choosing it.
Let's hope for the best.
It will definitely be a traditional Albanian symbol...
I think he's got it.
Beautiful.
Beautiful job, man.
I'm very happy to have a tattoo from you.
There is always a question that I ask to everyone around the world
to understand how happiness can change.
I would like to understand what happiness is for you,
after everything you've been through.
This is a big question...
Yeah...
A nice question...
Family is a big happiness.
Until getting married, having children,
you always think happiness is something else.
It can be prostitutes, football, drugs, alcohol...
Because you are not married.
Once you're married it's the happiest thing in your life.
It was nice meeting you guys.
Anytime you pass by you are welcome to stop by.
If you don't find me here, don't look for me,
because I'll be gone for good.
Either Heaven or Hell.
Most likely... People say this is Heaven
but when I go, I'll try Hell.
Because I've been to Heaven before, if that's it.
I wonder if Keq is aware of what he created.
Transforming a fallout bunker,
a symbol of oppression and fear,
into a welcoming place full of art, creativity and openness.
It's amazing how the Albanian people,
in such a dark time, were able to recognize
in art, in religion, and in a simple book,
the ways out and the seeds
to make a different reality blossom.
In these days I've learned to admire a nation
that I knew, but didn't understand.
Where high mountains protect from all sides
the traditions of a land among the proudest in the world.
I got the impression that Albanians had different eyes,
as if they had the innate ability
to find opportunities for happiness often invisible.
The emblem of Albanian happiness
I found in Beznic's words, which I leave you with:
If you don't love others, you don't love life.
How can you not be happy,
with all of this happiness that surrounds you?
There are rivers that flow, the mountains,
the sea, the clouds, the blue...
there are songs that remind you of all this...
My God...
Life is worth living!
ENGLISH SUBTITLES: Kayla Baker
